case_name: Thiodicarb removal from contaminated well water for drinking water supply
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for thiodicarb-contaminated well water intended for drinking water production.

input_data.scenario_background: A small community well serving a rural drinking water system has been impacted by nearby agricultural pesticide use, with thiodicarb identified as the primary organic contaminant of concern. The utility operates simple conventional treatment equipment and can add moderate pretreatment and adsorption steps, but it does not want highly specialized or energy-intensive systems. Raw water is otherwise typical of groundwater and has low turbidity, so the main treatment objective is reliable removal of the pesticide while maintaining practical operation.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve strong reduction of thiodicarb to a level consistent with protective drinking water practice and provide stable potable water quality.
secondary_goals: use proven treatment technology for pesticide control

core_unit_functions.cartridge_or_pressure_prefiltration: remove incidental particulates and protect downstream adsorption media
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb thiodicarb and other trace organic pesticide compounds from groundwater
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain fines and provide polished finished water
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in the distributed drinking water

applicability_rationale: thiodicarb is an organic pesticide, and activated carbon is a conventional, well-established barrier for many synthetic organic contaminants in drinking water treatment
the well water has low turbidity and low natural organic matter, which supports effective carbon adsorption and reasonable media life
the utility prefers proven and moderate-complexity treatment, and GAC is widely used for pesticide and taste-and-odor type organics under municipal operating conditions
this process chain avoids unnecessary high energy demand while directly targeting the identified contaminant
potential_risks: carbon breakthrough can occur if bed life is not monitored with appropriate sampling and replacement schedules
other trace organics not fully characterized could shorten GAC run length
poor prefiltration or hydraulic channeling may reduce adsorption performance

core_unit_functions.oxidant_dose_for_AOP_precursor: provide oxidant needed to generate reactive species during advanced oxidation
core_unit_functions.ultraviolet_advanced_oxidation: degrade thiodicarb and transform residual pesticide molecules
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: capture residual parent compound and oxidation byproducts that remain adsorbable

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can be effective for some pesticides and offers an additional destructive barrier when direct adsorption alone is uncertain
a polishing GAC step is prudent because oxidation may form transformation products that still require removal
the train is technically strong for trace organic control and finished-water assurance
potential_risks: higher energy use and process control burden than needed for a straightforward single-contaminant groundwater case
incomplete oxidation can create byproducts that require downstream adsorption verification
operator attention to oxidant dose, UV intensity, and quenching conditions is required

core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_addition: provide adsorptive capture of dissolved thiodicarb during short-term contact
core_unit_functions.rapid_mix_and_contact: disperse PAC and allow adsorption onto carbon particles
core_unit_functions.pressure_filtration: remove PAC and associated captured contaminant

applicability_rationale: PAC is a recognized conventional option for episodic or moderate organic contamination and can be integrated into existing filtration-based systems
this approach may be attractive where the contamination event is variable or seasonal and full-time GAC beds are less preferred
the raw well water has low solids, so filtration load from PAC handling remains manageable
potential_risks: short contact time can limit removal compared with fixed-bed GAC, especially for persistent dissolved organics
chemical feed variability can cause inconsistent treatment performance
spent PAC handling and solids production are greater than with simple fixed-bed adsorption

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide broad rejection of dissolved organic contaminants including pesticide molecules
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry to reduce corrosivity and improve finished water quality
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure biological safety after treatment and storage

applicability_rationale: pressure membranes can achieve strong removal of a wide range of organic micropollutants
this option may be technically attractive if future multi-contaminant groundwater issues are anticipated
potential_risks: concentrate management is a major drawback for a small well-water system
membrane fouling control and post-treatment stabilization increase complexity
this is more treatment intensity than is typically justified for a single pesticide problem under moderate constraints

core_unit_functions.air_stripping: transfer volatile compounds from water to air
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: polish water and remove incidental particulates

applicability_rationale: included as a low-ranking comparator because aeration is sometimes considered for organic chemicals in groundwater treatment reviews
it is operationally simple in some settings
potential_risks: thiodicarb is not a strongly volatile contaminant, so air stripping is not expected to be an effective primary removal technology
selection of this process could lead to underperformance against drinking water goals
off-gas considerations would still apply if any stripping occurred
